title: Savers Value Village, Inc. Q4 2025 Earnings Call Summary
content:
Savers Value Village, Inc. Q4 2025 Earnings Call Summary - Moby

Strategic Performance Drivers

Achieved the first quarter of year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth in nearly two years, marking a strategic inflection point as new store cohorts begin to mature. U.S. comparable sales growth of 8.8% was driven by a secular shift toward thrift, with customer demographics skewing younger and more affluent (45% of U.S. shoppers have HHI above $100,000). Canadian operations stabilized with 0.7% comps, where management's conservative production management and disciplined labor allocation successfully grew segment profit despite macro headwinds. New store performance remains consistent with the long-term model of reaching a 20% contribution margin by year five, with recent openings performing in line with internal expectations. Strategic capital allocation prioritized high-return store expansion while simultaneously reducing annual interest expense by $17 million through a new capital structure. Innovation initiatives, including 'ABP Lite' for automated book processing, are being deployed to 85% of the fleet to drive operational efficiency and offset wage inflation.

2026 Outlook and Strategic Assumptions

Guidance for 2026 assumes adjusted EBITDA growth with roughly flat margins, reflecting the transition of the 2024 store class into their more profitable third year of operation. Management is planning the Canadian business conservatively with flat to low single-digit comps, assuming no material improvement in the regional macro environment. The 2026 store opening plan of approximately 25 units will shift focus heavily toward the U.S., including entry into new markets in North Carolina and Tennessee. Pre-opening expenses of $14 million to $16 million will be more front-loaded in 2026 compared to 2025 due to a more balanced opening cadence across the first three quarters. Long-term financial targets remain focused on achieving high teens adjusted EBITDA margins as the total store pipeline reaches full maturity.

Operational Risks and Structural Adjustments

The 53rd week in fiscal 2025 creates a 2% headwind to total sales growth comparisons in 2026, though it is expected to be bottom-line neutral. IPO-related stock-based compensation is expected to fully run off by the end of the first half of 2026, providing a modest tailwind to operating expense leverage. Severe weather in January 2024 disrupted early Q1 trends in both the U.S. and Canada, though management noted a 'nice rebound' in February. An earlier Easter holiday is expected to negatively impact Canadian Q1 comps by approximately 1 percentage point due to mandatory store closures on Good Friday.

Story Continues

Q&A Session Highlights

Our analysts just identified a stock with the potential to be the next Nvidia. Tell us how you invest and we'll show you why it's our #1 pick. Tap here.

Drivers of margin expansion sustainability beyond 2025

Management explained that margin expansion is primarily a 'math equation' driven by the maturation of stores entering years three through five of their lifecycle. Efficiency gains from the innovation agenda and a significant deceleration of store openings in the lower-growth Canadian market will also support margin accretion.

Pricing strategy and competitive value gaps

The company targets price increases slightly below the rate of inflation to maintain a compelling value gap against discount retail competitors. Management views price increases by competitors as an opportunity to gain market share rather than a signal to aggressively raise their own prices.

Supply chain dynamics in new greenfield markets

New market entries are predicated on robust 'on-site donation' (OSD) potential, which management considers the highest quality and lowest cost supply source. The company utilizes a 'tool belt' of supply options, including GreenDrop and non-profit deliveries, to supplement OSD in new regions like North Carolina.

title: Savers Value Village outlines plan for 25 new stores and $1.76B–$1.79B in 2026 net sales amid U.S. thrift momentum
content:
Earnings Call Insights: Savers Value Village (SVV) Q4 2025

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* Mark Walsh, CEO, reported "We delivered our anticipated inflection in earnings, posting our first quarter of year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth in nearly 2 years, supported by profit contribution gains in both countries." He highlighted U.S. sales growth of 20.6% and comparable sales up 8.8%, attributing this to "accelerating consumer adoption of thrift and stellar execution by our team, delivering compelling value to consumers." In Canada, Walsh stated trends have stabilized and a conservative approach to planning led to year-over-year segment profit growth. The company opened 10 new stores in the quarter, ending the year with 26 openings. Walsh said, "We remain confident in our long-term store growth opportunity and a targeted 20% store level contribution margin." The innovation agenda was also emphasized, including ABP Lite automation and AI-enabled efficiency initiatives. Walsh closed by noting a new capital structure that "reduces annual interest expense by $17 million and provides flexibility for continued debt reduction." 
 	* Michael Maher, CFO, stated, "Total net sales increased 15.6% to $465 million. Excluding the benefit of the 53rd week, total net sales increased 8.4%." He noted U.S. net sales reached $266 million, with comparable store sales up 8.8%. Maher also reported, "Adjusted net income was $24 million or $0.15 per diluted share. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $74 million, and adjusted EBITDA margin was 15.9%." He emphasized a strong balance sheet with $86 million in cash and a net leverage ratio of 2.5x, with repayment of $20 million in debt and repurchase of 1.1 million shares. Maher stated, "Our strong cash flow generation will enable us to further deleverage our business as we target a net leverage ratio of under 2x within the next couple of years."

OUTLOOK

	* The company issued 2026 guidance for net sales of $1.76 billion to $1.79 billion, comparable store sales growth of 2.5% to 4%, net income of $66 million to $78 million or $0.41 to $0.48 per diluted share, adjusted net income of $73 million to $85 million or $0.45 to $0.53 per diluted share, and adjusted EBITDA of $260 million to $275 million. Maher said, "We expect new store openings to be reasonably balanced between the first and second half of the year, with most occurring in the second and third quarters." The company plans approximately 25 new store openings, with over 20 in the U.S., and expects modest improvement in gross profit margins as new store headwinds abate. Maher explained, "We are assuming no material change in the U.S. or Canadian economies in 2026."

FINANCIAL RESULTS

	* U.S. net sales increased 20.6% to $266 million, while Canadian net sales increased 9.1% to $156 million. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $74 million, with a margin of 15.9%. U.S. segment profit was $60 million, up $11 million, and Canada segment profit was $43 million, up $4 million. Cost of merchandise sold as a percentage of net sales increased 30 basis points to 44.6%. Salaries, wages and benefits expense was $93 million, and SG&A increased 8% to $99 million but decreased as a percentage of net sales to 21.4%. Depreciation and amortization increased 32% to $22 million. GAAP net income for the quarter was $22 million or $0.14 per diluted share.

Q&A

 	* Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Questioned U.S. comp progression post-holidays and Q1 trend. Michael Maher: Answered, "For the quarter-to-date, we're continuing to see strength in the U.S., and Canada remains up slightly."
 	* Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Asked about pricing strategy and market share. Walsh: Replied, "If others do raise price, we do think it's an opportunity for us to gain share, absolutely."
 	* Mark Altschwager, Baird: Inquired about customer trends and regional differences. Walsh: Noted, "We're really excited about... the continued growth of our younger customers and the continued growth of more affluent customers or trade down."
 	* Dylan Carden, William Blair: Asked about EBITDA cadence. Maher: Clarified, "Q1 is flat to slightly up EBITDA dollars... preopening expenses are more front-loaded because our new store openings are more even across the year."
 	* Robert Drbul, BTIG: Questioned new market supply and store pipeline. Jubran Tanious, COO: Stated, "We will not open a new store unless we feel good about that supply equation."
 	* Alexia Morgan, Piper Sandler: Asked about margin expansion despite new stores. Maher: Explained, "The biggest thing is the continued maturation of new stores."

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts maintained a positive tone, focusing on U.S. momentum, margin expansion, and new store productivity, with questions probing for further upside and risk factors.
 	* Management expressed high confidence, frequently referencing "momentum" and "broad-based gains," and used assertive phrases such as "We are very pleased" and "We remain confident."
 	* Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased optimism, with less focus on macro headwinds and more on growth execution and innovation.

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* Guidance for 2026 net sales and EBITDA is higher than previous guidance for 2025, reflecting increased confidence in U.S. growth and new store maturation.
 	* Strategic focus shifted further toward U.S. expansion, with 20+ new U.S. stores planned and more balanced store openings throughout the year.
 	* Analysts focused on margin progression and store pipeline, while management highlighted technology initiatives and innovation.
 	* Management’s tone was more confident, citing an "inflection in profitability" and progress on innovation. Analysts expressed greater comfort with growth and margin outlook, compared to the prior quarter's focus on macro risks and Canadian headwinds.

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Management noted continued conservative planning for Canada due to "unchanged" macro conditions and does not assume "any material improvement in the Canadian economy."
 	* Preopening expenses for new stores remain a headwind, estimated at $14 million to $16 million in 2026.
 	* Weather disruptions in January affected U.S. and Canadian business, but recent rebound was reported.
 	* No concerns about supply for new stores, as stated by Tanious: "No concerns there."

FINAL TAKEAWAY

Savers Value Village management highlighted a return to adjusted EBITDA growth, robust U.S. momentum, and a confident expansion strategy with 25 new stores planned for 2026. Guidance reflects strong confidence in continued margin improvement and capital allocation discipline, while ongoing innovation and efficiency initiatives aim to support profitability as the company balances new store investments and macro uncertainties in Canada.

title: Savers Value Village Inc (SVV) Q4 2025 Earnings Call Highlights: Strong US Sales Drive ...
content:
This article first appeared on GuruFocus.

Total Net Sales: Increased 15.6% to $465 million; 8.4% increase excluding the 53rd week. US Net Sales: Increased 20.6% to $266 million; 12.6% increase excluding the 53rd week. Comparable Store Sales: Increased 5.4% overall; 8.8% in the US and 0.7% in Canada. Adjusted EBITDA: $74 million, representing 15.9% of sales. Net Income: GAAP net income of $22 million or $0.14 per diluted share; Adjusted net income of $24 million or $0.15 per diluted share. Store Openings: 10 new stores in the quarter, totaling 26 new store openings for 2025. Cash and Cash Equivalents: $86 million with a net leverage ratio of 2.5 times. Debt Repayment: $20 million repaid during the quarter. Share Repurchase: 1.1 million shares repurchased at a weighted average price of $8.75. 2026 Guidance: Net sales of $1.76 billion to $1.79 billion; Comparable store sales growth of 2.5% to 4%; Adjusted EBITDA of $260 million to $275 million.

Warning! GuruFocus has detected 2 Warning Sign with SVV. Is SVV fairly valued? Test your thesis with our free DCF calculator.

Release Date: February 19, 2026

For the complete transcript of the earnings call, please refer to the full earnings call transcript.

Positive Points

Savers Value Village Inc (NYSE:SVV) reported a 15.6% increase in total net sales for the fourth quarter, reaching $465 million. The US segment showed strong performance with a 20.6% increase in net sales, driven by an 8.8% increase in comparable store sales. The company opened 26 new stores in 2025 and plans to open around 25 new stores in 2026, focusing on expansion in the US. SVV's loyalty program has grown to 6.1 million active members, indicating strong customer engagement. The company successfully reduced its annual interest expense by $17 million through a new capital structure, enhancing financial flexibility.

Negative Points

The Canadian market showed modest growth with only a 0.7% increase in comparable store sales, reflecting a conservative business approach due to macroeconomic conditions. Salaries, wages, and benefits expenses increased by 90 basis points to 19.2% of net sales, driven by new store growth and higher wage rates. The company anticipates $14 to $16 million in pre-opening expenses for new stores in 2026, which could impact short-term profitability. SVV is planning for flat to low single-digit comparable store sales growth in Canada, indicating limited growth expectations in that market. The company faces a 2% headwind to total sales growth in 2026 due to lapping a 53-week fiscal year in 2025.

Q & A Highlights

Q: Could you speak to the progression of same-store sales post-holiday in the US and how it compares to the fourth quarter momentum? A: Michael Maher, CFO: We have continued to see good momentum in the US, despite some disruptions due to severe weather in January. February has shown a nice rebound, and we feel good about the trends relative to our guidance for Q1.

Story Continues

Q: With the acceleration in new store growth in the US, could you elaborate on new store productivity and expected returns? A: Michael Maher, CFO: New stores are progressing in line with expectations, averaging around $3 million in sales in the first year and ramping up to $5 million by the fifth year. They typically break even by year two and reach a 20% contribution margin by year five.

Q: What are your latest thoughts on pricing, especially as the industry has raised prices recently? A: Mark Walsh, CEO: We monitor pricing relative to competition and aim to deliver a compelling price-value relationship. We see opportunities to gain market share if others raise prices, and we target price increases slightly below inflation.

Q: Can you discuss the inflection back to gross profit margin expansion expected in 2026? A: Michael Maher, CFO: The expansion will be modest, driven by the maturation of new stores and efficiency initiatives. We expect continued margin improvement in Canada even with low growth, supported by efficiency gains and a slowdown in new store openings.

Q: How should we think about the goal for annual margin leverage with mid-single-digit comp and high-single-digit revenue growth? A: Michael Maher, CFO: We expect profit margins to follow profit dollars as new stores mature. Our long-term target is a high-teens adjusted EBITDA margin, with continued tailwinds from maturing stores.

Q: Are there any shifts in the source of supply, such as in-store versus delivered by nonprofits? A: Jubran Tanious, COO: No significant changes have been observed. We continue to see robust on-site donation growth, which we expect to continue in 2026 and beyond.

Q: Do you think the consumer behavior of leaning into thrift for holiday gifting is sticky and could carry over to other holidays? A: Mark Walsh, CEO: We see low attrition rates in our loyalty database, indicating stickiness. The strong fourth-quarter comps suggest broader consumer adoption and acceptance of thrift for gifting.

Q: Can you provide insights into the brand recognition in the US compared to Canada? A: Mark Walsh, CEO: Brand recognition varies by market, with strong recognition in mature stores. In new markets, we are gaining recognition rapidly, as evidenced by new store performance.

title: Savers Value Village, Inc. Reports Fourth Quarter Financial Results
content:
Net sales increased 15.6%, or 8.4% in constant currency1 excluding the 53rd week

Comparable store sales increased 5.4%; U.S. up 8.8% and Canada up 0.7%

Earnings in line with prior outlook; strong operating performance in the quarter

Provides fiscal 2026 outlook

BELLEVUE, Wash., February 19, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--Savers Value Village, Inc. (NYSE: SVV), (the "Company") today announced financial results for the fourteen weeks ended January 3, 2026 (the "fourth quarter"). The Company’s results for both the fourth quarter and full year ended January 3, 2026 included the benefit of one additional week (the "53rd week") relative to the prior year comparative periods.

Highlights for the Fourth Quarter; Comparisons are to the thirteen weeks ended December 28, 2024

Total Company net sales increased 15.6% to $464.7 million. Excluding the benefit of the 53rd week, net sales increased 8.4%, constant-currency net sales1 increased 8.4% and comparable store sales increased 5.4%. For the United States ("U.S."), net sales increased 20.6%. Excluding the benefit of the 53rd week, net sales increased 12.6% and comparable store sales increased 8.8%. For Canada, net sales increased 9.1%. Excluding the benefit of the 53rd week, net sales increased 3.1%, constant-currency net sales1 increased 3.0% and comparable store sales increased 0.7%. The Company opened 10 new stores, ending the fourth quarter with 367 stores. For the fifty-three weeks ended January 3, 2026 ("fiscal 2025"), the Company opened a total of 26 new stores. Net income was $22.4 million, or $0.14 per diluted share. Net income margin was 4.8%. Adjusted net income1 was $23.8 million, or $0.15 per diluted share. Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization ("Adjusted EBITDA")1 was $74.1 million and Adjusted EBITDA margin1 was 15.9%. Changes in foreign currency exchange rates positively impacted Adjusted EBITDA1 by $0.1 million during the fourth quarter. The Company repaid $20 million of principal under its 2025 term loan facility and repurchased approximately 1.1 million shares of its common stock at a weighted average price of $8.75, excluding commissions, pursuant to its share repurchase programs. As of January 3, 2026, the Company had approximately $41.7 million remaining under its share repurchase program.

Mark Walsh, Chief Executive Officer of Savers Value Village, Inc. stated, "We are proud of our fourth quarter results, as we delivered a clear inflection in profitability driven by strong U.S. demand, stabilizing trends in Canada, and the contribution from our maturing new stores. These fundamentals give us confidence that we are well-positioned to continue delivering EBITDA growth in 2026 and beyond."

Story Continues

1 Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin, as well as amounts presented on a constant currency basis, are not measures recognized under U.S. generally accepted accounting principles ("GAAP"). For additional information on our use of non-GAAP financial measures, see "Non-GAAP Financial Measures", "Constant Currency" and the accompanying financial tables which reconcile GAAP financial measures to these non-GAAP measures.

Stores Update

The following unaudited table summarizes the Company’s store count activity for fiscal 2025:

Non-GAAP Financial Measures

The Company reports its financial results in accordance with GAAP. Non-GAAP financial measures used by the Company include Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin. The Company has included these non-GAAP financial measures in this press release as they are key measures used by its management and its board of directors to evaluate its operating performance and the effectiveness of its business strategies, make budgeting decisions, and evaluate compensation decisions. Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin are not calculated or presented in accordance with GAAP and have limitations as analytical tools. You should not consider them in isolation, as a substitute for, or superior to, analysis of the Company’s results as reported under GAAP. There are limitations to using non-GAAP financial measures, including those amounts presented in accordance with the Company’s definitions of Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin, as they may not be comparable to similar measures disclosed by the Company’s competitors, because not all companies and analysts calculate Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin in the same manner. Because of these limitations, you should consider Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin alongside other financial performance measures, including, as applicable, net income (loss) and the Company’s other GAAP results. The Company presents Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin because it considers these meaningful measures to share with investors as they best allow comparison of the performance of one period with that of another period. In addition, by presenting Adjusted net income, Adjusted net income per diluted share, Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EBITDA margin, the Company provides investors with management’s perspective of the Company’s operating performance.

The Company defines Adjusted net income as net income (loss) excluding the impact of loss on extinguishment of debt, IPO-related stock-based compensation expense, transaction costs, foreign currency exchange rate impacts, executive transition costs, certain other adjustments, the tax effect on the above adjustments and the excess tax (benefit) shortfall from stock-based compensation. The Company defines Adjusted net income per diluted share as Adjusted net income divided by diluted weighted average common shares outstanding.

The Company defines Adjusted EBITDA as net income (loss) excluding the impact of interest expense, net, income tax expense, depreciation and amortization, loss on extinguishment of debt, stock-based compensation expense, lease intangible asset expense, executive transition costs, transaction costs, foreign currency exchange rate impacts and certain other adjustments. The Company defines Adjusted EBITDA margin as Adjusted EBITDA divided by net sales, expressed as a percentage.

Constant Currency

The Company reports certain operating results on a constant-currency basis in order to facilitate period-to-period comparisons of its results without regard to the impact of fluctuating foreign currency exchange rates. The term foreign currency exchange rates refers to the exchange rates used to translate the Company's operating results for all countries where the functional currency is not the USD into the USD. Because the Company is a global company, foreign currency exchange rates used for translation may have a significant effect on its reported results. In general, given the Company's significant operations in Canada, the Company's financial results are affected positively by a weakening of the USD against the CAD and are affected negatively by a strengthening of the USD against the CAD. References to operating results on a constant-currency basis indicate operating results without the impact of foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations.

The Company believes disclosure of constant-currency net sales is helpful to investors because it facilitates period-to-period comparisons of its results by increasing the transparency of its underlying performance by excluding the impact of fluctuating foreign currency exchange rates. However, constant-currency results are not calculated or presented in accordance with GAAP and are not meant to be considered as an alternative or substitute for, or superior to, comparable measures prepared in accordance with GAAP. Constant-currency results have no standardized meaning prescribed by GAAP, are not prepared under any comprehensive set of accounting rules or principles and should be read in conjunction with the Company's consolidated financial statements prepared in accordance with GAAP.

Constant-currency results have limitations in their usefulness to investors and may be calculated differently from, and therefore may not be directly comparable to, similarly titled measures used by other companies.

Constant-currency information compares results between periods as if exchange rates had remained constant period-over-period. During the fourteen weeks ended January 3, 2026, as compared to the thirteen weeks ended December 28, 2024, the USD was weaker relative to the CAD and the Australian dollar ("AUD") which resulted in a favorable foreign currency impact on our operating results. During the fifty-three weeks ended January 3, 2026, as compared to the fifty-two weeks ended December 28, 2024, the USD was stronger relative to the CAD and the AUD which resulted in an unfavorable foreign currency impact on our operating results. The Company calculates constant-currency net sales by translating current period net sales using the average exchange rates from the comparative prior period rather than the actual average exchange rates in effect.

title: 4 Consumer Discretionary Stocks Set to Win This Earnings Season
content:
The Consumer Discretionary sector has entered the earnings season with momentum that is stronger than many expected. While the consumer discretionary sector spans a broad mix of industries from autos to travel and home improvement, our focus here is on lifestyle-driven businesses, including apparel brands, footwear manufacturers, fragrance companies and accessories players. These categories sit at the intersection of fashion, identity and aspiration, making them especially sensitive to shifts in consumer sentiment, disposable income and trend cycles.

This corner of the sector is showing renewed strength. Consumers are prioritizing brands that deliver authenticity, innovation, compelling price points and emotional connection, rather than simply chasing discounts. Shoppers are willing to pay for differentiated design, comfort technology, statement accessories and signature scents. At the same time, companies have become more disciplined, tightening inventory, refining assortments and leveraging data analytics to better match supply with demand.

Digital engagement continues to be a powerful growth lever. Robust direct-to-consumer platforms, loyalty ecosystems and influencer-led marketing are enabling lifestyle brands to deepen customer relationships while capturing higher margins. Accessories, in particular, are benefiting from trend-driven purchases and higher repeat buying behavior, supporting both top-line momentum and profitability. Meanwhile, easing input and freight costs are providing incremental relief, setting the stage for potential margin expansion.

However, the Consumer Discretionary sector is not without its challenges. Lifestyle-focused Consumer Discretionary companies remain exposed to macro volatility, including uneven global demand, cautious consumer spending and currency fluctuations in key international markets. Even as inflation moderates, shoppers are still value-conscious, which can pressure pricing power and elevate promotional activity. Fast-changing fashion trends and shorter product cycles increase execution risks, particularly for apparel, footwear and accessories brands that must balance inventory carefully to avoid markdowns.

As earnings season unfolds, apparel makers, footwear brands, fragrance houses and accessories companies that combine brand heat with operational discipline are especially well positioned. Those demonstrating pricing power, efficient inventory management and compelling product pipelines could deliver upside surprises. In a sector where perception and momentum matter, lifestyle-focused consumer discretionary stocks may emerge as standout performers this reporting cycle.

Earnings Expectations This Season

The Zacks Consumer Discretionary sector looks poised for growth in this earnings season, owing to the aforementioned trends and initiatives of players. Carter’s is the largest marketer of branded apparel and related products for babies and young children in North America. Carter’s demonstrates momentum across both its U.S. Retail and International segments, underscoring steady consumer engagement and expanding global relevance. Carter’s continues to leverage strategic pricing actions to mitigate rising costs and sustain profitability amid tariff pressures. Despite inflationary conditions, Carter’s compelling value proposition, anchored by affordable core products, continues to resonate with budget-conscious families, particularly younger Gen Z parents.

title: Savers Value Village (SVV) Earnings Expected to Grow: Should You Buy?
content:
Savers Value Village (SVV) is expected to deliver a year-over-year increase in earnings on higher revenues when it reports results for the quarter ended December 2025. This widely-known consensus outlook gives a good sense of the company's earnings picture, but how the actual results compare to these estimates is a powerful factor that could impact its near-term stock price.

The stock might move higher if these key numbers top expectations in the upcoming earnings report, which is expected to be released on February 19. On the other hand, if they miss, the stock may move lower.

While the sustainability of the immediate price change and future earnings expectations will mostly depend on management's discussion of business conditions on the earnings call, it's worth handicapping the probability of a positive EPS surprise.

Zacks Consensus Estimate

This retailer of second-hand merchandise is expected to post quarterly earnings of $0.16 per share in its upcoming report, which represents a year-over-year change of +60%.

Revenues are expected to be $464.69 million, up 15.6% from the year-ago quarter.

Estimate Revisions Trend

The consensus EPS estimate for the quarter has remained unchanged over the last 30 days. This is essentially a reflection of how the covering analysts have collectively reassessed their initial estimates over this period.

Investors should keep in mind that the direction of estimate revisions by each of the covering analysts may not always get reflected in the aggregate change.

Price, Consensus and EPS SurprisePrice, Consensus and EPS Surprise Chart for SVV

Earnings Whisper

Estimate revisions ahead of a company's earnings release offer clues to the business conditions for the period whose results are coming out. Our proprietary surprise prediction model -- the Zacks Earnings ESP (Expected Surprise Prediction) -- has this insight at its core.

The Zacks Earnings ESP compares the Most Accurate Estimate to the Zacks Consensus Estimate for the quarter; the Most Accurate Estimate is a more recent version of the Zacks Consensus EPS estimate. The idea here is that analysts revising their estimates right before an earnings release have the latest information, which could potentially be more accurate than what they and others contributing to the consensus had predicted earlier.

Thus, a positive or negative Earnings ESP reading theoretically indicates the likely deviation of the actual earnings from the consensus estimate. However, the model's predictive power is significant for positive ESP readings only.

A positive Earnings ESP is a strong predictor of an earnings beat, particularly when combined with a Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy), 2 (Buy) or 3 (Hold). Our research shows that stocks with this combination produce a positive surprise nearly 70% of the time, and a solid Zacks Rank actually increases the predictive power of Earnings ESP.

Story Continues

Please note that a negative Earnings ESP reading is not indicative of an earnings miss. Our research shows that it is difficult to predict an earnings beat with any degree of confidence for stocks with negative Earnings ESP readings and/or Zacks Rank of 4 (Sell) or 5 (Strong Sell).

How Have the Numbers Shaped Up for Savers Value?

For Savers Value, the Most Accurate Estimate is higher than the Zacks Consensus Estimate, suggesting that analysts have recently become bullish on the company's earnings prospects. This has resulted in an Earnings ESP of +1.08%.

On the other hand, the stock currently carries a Zacks Rank of #3.

So, this combination indicates that Savers Value will most likely beat the consensus EPS estimate.

Does Earnings Surprise History Hold Any Clue?

Analysts often consider to what extent a company has been able to match consensus estimates in the past while calculating their estimates for its future earnings. So, it's worth taking a look at the surprise history for gauging its influence on the upcoming number.

For the last reported quarter, it was expected that Savers Value would post earnings of $0.14 per share when it actually produced earnings of $0.14, delivering no surprise.

Over the last four quarters, the company has beaten consensus EPS estimates two times.

Bottom Line

An earnings beat or miss may not be the sole basis for a stock moving higher or lower. Many stocks end up losing ground despite an earnings beat due to other factors that disappoint investors. Similarly, unforeseen catalysts help a number of stocks gain despite an earnings miss.

That said, betting on stocks that are expected to beat earnings expectations does increase the odds of success. This is why it's worth checking a company's Earnings ESP and Zacks Rank ahead of its quarterly release. Make sure to utilize our Earnings ESP Filter to uncover the best stocks to buy or sell before they've reported.

Savers Value appears a compelling earnings-beat candidate. However, investors should pay attention to other factors too for betting on this stock or staying away from it ahead of its earnings release.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value using a required rate of return.

Digital transformation drives growth, with investments in personalization, mobile, omnichannel and fulfillment enhancing consumer engagement. The company has been adding consumers to its direct-to-consumer business, highlighting the effectiveness of its strategies and the strong appeal of its products, which is expected to have aided the fiscal third-quarter performance. Such positives are anticipated to be reflected in its top and bottom-line results.

Retail and wholesale have been the key pillars, with flagship stores, premium distribution and partnerships expected to have boosted comparable sales across North America, Europe and Asia in third-quarter fiscal 2026. 

On the last reported quarter’s earnings call, management was optimistic about its continued brand momentum and outperformance across all regions and channels in the first half of the year.

title: Why Savers Value Village Stock Popped This Week
content:
Key Points

Management stated that sales would rise by 8% this year, while adjusted earnings per share would be $0.45. After sales growth dipped in 2024, this is a welcome reacceleration for shareholders. Savers Value Village offers an intriguing growth story as it expands into the Southeastern U.S. 10 stocks we like better than Savers Value Village ›

Savers Value Village (NYSE: SVV) -- the largest for-profit thrift retailer in North America -- initially rose as much as 12% this week before giving back a portion of these returns as the week ended. The catalyst for this higher share price came from a preliminary earnings announcement that projected sales and earnings figures that easily outpaced Wall Street's expectations. For the fourth quarter, management expects 8% sales growth (excluding the benefit of this year's 53rd week on their fiscal calendar) and a 5% comparable-store sales increase. Management also reiterated 2025 projections of roughly $0.45 in adjusted earnings per share, leaving the stock trading at 23 times earnings at today's price.

Savers Value Village's sales growth reaccelerates

While Savers Value Village is a for-profit thrift retailer -- as opposed to a nonprofit charitable organization like its primary mega-peer -- it directly supports nonprofits by purchasing donated clothes and household goods from these charities to resell at a discounted rate. Home to 367 stores across the United States, Canada, and Australia, Savers Value Village counts roughly six million customers a loyalty members. The company's store count is up from 326 in 2023, and management expects to open 25 new stores in 2026 -- with ample room to continue growing throughout the U.S., particularly.Image source: Getty Images.

With the second-hand apparel market growing roughly five times faster than the broader clothing industry -- and projected to grow by 9% annually through 2029 -- Savers Value Village is well-positioned to benefit from the megatrend. After sales growth dipped to 1% in late 2024, the company's growth has reaccelerated and could be here to stay as it expands into largely untapped markets such as Texas, Florida, and other Southeastern U.S. states. I'll be keeping an eye on this intriguing stock, especially if higher growth persists.
